title: 'They want us gone' - Man Utd fans' fury over ticket ultimatum
description: Fans tell BBC Sport that a crackdown on touting is affecting some of the club's most loyal fans.
fullText:
Manchester United fans have reacted with fury after "thousands" had their season-ticket accounts restricted.

The action follows an attempt by United to identify potential ticket touts and those breaking the club's ticketing terms and conditions.

But supporters have told BBC Sport that those who have been informed their season tickets are now at risk of being revoked include many of the club's most loyal followers, and insist that the majority have done nothing wrong.

"I feel angry about it - they just want us gone," Andy, a season ticket holder for more than 35 years, who was informed his account has been restricted on Monday night, told BBC Sport.

"My dad has been a season-ticket holder since 1974, when we were in the second division. He is a pensioner now and I have to help him with all the online stuff since digital tickets became a thing.

"Going the match is our bond. Things like that are what keep older people alive sometimes.

"It's stressful enough for my own mental health but I think it could make my dad ill."

Manchester United declined to comment on the process or fan reaction when asked to do so.

In one email sent to a supporter, seen by BBC Sport, Manchester United said: "We have identified patterns linked to your ticketing account that we need to understand more clearly.

"We need to establish whether there is a straightforward explanation for the activity identified."

As it stands, those under suspicion have had restrictions placed their online accounts, preventing them from buying and transferring tickets.

Those whose accounts have been flagged have been asked to submit evidence to the club to defend themselves if they feel they have been wrongly accused.

United have not revoked any tickets so far and an appeals panel will make a final decision on each case before their Premier League season begins on 22 August.

But supporters such as Andy say they have not been told explicitly what they are being accused of, that the lack of information feels like they are being invited to incriminate themselves, and that the confusion is causing significant anxiety.

"They have already taken my season ticket money," Andy says.

"They don't care about how it affects people, their relationships, maybe their health and mental health. There is no empathy.

"I feel like people are punished for their loyalty. They don't want fans, they want customers."

In a social media post, Manchester United Supporters' Trust said the club has "been sending out account suspensions and season-ticket cancellation notices to thousands of supporters".

"We have been overwhelmed with requests for help [and] have sent out individual advice to hundreds," the trust added.

"Clearly the scale of this has caused huge concern among fans.

"We are increasingly concerned that the current approach risks causing significant unintended consequences."

Physical season tickets are a thing of the past in the Premier League.

Clubs now use digital systems where fans download tickets to their phones. The change has been accompanied at many clubs by stricter rules on how, and how often, ticket holders can transfer their tickets to other people.

At Manchester United, for example, fans can have their season ticket revoked if they do not attend or forward their ticket to approved friends and family members for at least 16 out of 19 Premier League fixtures.

Last season, the club moved 500 season ticket holders from seats they had used for years in order to accommodate an increase in hospitality tickets.

The Red Devils have made sweeping cuts to their workforce since the partial takeover by Ineos in 2024, including those working in ticketing.

The method of identifying suspicious accounts United have employed uses third-party software and is largely automated.

In a message sent to the club fans' forum last week, after the first restrictions were sent out to supporters, United defended their approach and insisted the vast majority of those facing potential sanctions have misbehaved.

"Our recent operation focused on instances where multiple accounts displayed patterns of activity indicating they may have been centrally controlled, coordinated by the same individual, or operated using automated software," they said.

"We are confident that the vast majority of accounts identified for misuse have been correctly flagged with the appropriate action taken."

United conducted a crackdown on touting last season, cancelling 22,000 tickets for the first five home games of the season.

They regard that figure as "the tip of the iceberg" and believe around 10-15% of season tickets are controlled by touts.

"Every single one of our members with a season ticket has had the email," says the secretary of a United supporters' club in Northern Ireland - a fan for more than 50 years, who asked not to be named.

"If one of our members can't go to a game for some reason, they'll usually offer it to another member and lots of our accounts are linked together. Everything is fair and done for face value. But the club don't seem to understand that, or want to understand it.

"It's just so disappointing. Everybody is really angry."

Fans told BBC Sport they believe some of Manchester United's most loyal matchgoers have been erroneously targeted because they are more likely to ask friends or family members to log in to their account, for example to purchase tickets for away matches as a group or for help when busy with work or childcare, which they say the club is conflating with touting.

"It feels like you're not allowed to help your mates out and pass your ticket on to them, because the club will accuse you of touting," Andy says.

"But you can sell your ticket back to them and they'll sell it to someone else at a much higher price than you paid by adding on some free drinks or food.

Touting - illegally reselling match tickets at an inflated price - is a widespread issue in the Premier League.

Last season, BBC Sport exposed the black market of top-flight tickets available for resale online, which circumvent UK laws against ticket touting by being based abroad.

Manchester United insist that revoked season tickets will be repurposed as new ones for supporters on the waiting list.

But some fans believe that clubs - particularly the wealthiest - want fewer season-ticket holders in their stadiums, because selling seats for matches on an individual basis generates more revenue per ticket.

In theory, fans who are tourists visiting infrequently are more likely to spend money in the stadium and in club stores than regulars who attend every match.

The number of season-ticket holders at Old Trafford has dropped from 64,000 in 2006 to 46,800 in 2026.

"Season ticket rules are definitely a concern," says Thomas Concannon, Premier League network manager at the Football Supporters' Association.

"We've all benefited from passing a ticket on to a mate for face value, and fans worry that even something like that is now deemed against the rules. We need a proper consensus on the rules across all clubs.

"Taking a season ticket away harms the atmosphere and means you're losing that fan for life.

"We stand behind protecting season-ticket holders and having as many of them as possible."

title: Why was Hamilton penalised but not Leclerc? - F1 Q&A
description: BBC Sport F1 correspondent Andrew Benson answers your latest questions before the Hungarian Grand Prix.
fullText:
Mercedes' Kimi Antonelli won the Belgian Grand Prix from pole position on Sunday to extend his lead at the top of the drivers' championship to 45 points.

Antonelli's team-mate George Russell retired from the race following an opening-lap collision with Ferrari's Lewis Hamilton.

Russell has now dropped to third in the standings, with Hamilton replacing him in second place after finishing fourth.

Ferrari's Charles Leclerc was second behind Antonelli and Red Bull's Max Verstappen completed the podium in third.

BBC Sport F1 correspondent Andrew Benson answers your latest questions after the race at Spa-Francorchamps.

Antonelli near-perfect but too much drama for Hamilton - driver ratings

Russell 'numb to disappointment' after bitter blow to title hopes

If Lewis Hamilton received a five-second time penalty for what appeared to me to be a racing incident between himself and George Russell, then how did Charles Leclerc get away with the Oscar Piastri incident where he clearly didn't leave enough room for the McLaren driver? - John

The stewards' justifications for these two different incidents were based on a number of factors, many related to the Formula 1 drivers' guidelines.

In the case of the collision between Hamilton and Russell on the first lap, the decision stated that Russell had got his car far enough alongside Hamilton's Ferrari on the outside as they approached the corner to be making a legitimate overtaking attempt and therefore was "entitled to racing room".

They went on to say that the collision between the two cars was triggered by Hamilton's car sliding and that he hit Russell's car despite "applying corrective steering".

Russell "remained within the available racing room and the collision was caused by [Hamilton] being unable to maintain sufficient separation".

Hamilton was therefore "wholly at fault for the collision".

The normal penalty in these circumstances is 10 seconds. This was reduced to five in Hamilton's case because he "acknowledged the presence of [Russell], attempted to leave appropriate racing room and made a genuine attempt to avoid contact".

In addition, the incident was not caused by "an aggressive or deliberate manoeuvre", and it was on the opening lap, when more leeway is applied.

As for the incident between Leclerc and Piastri, again the racing rules were applied to the letter.

The stewards ruled that Piastri was not far enough alongside the Ferrari to be entitled to the same treatment as Russell.

This was because while he had "some overlap", Piastri's "front axle… was not ahead of the front axle [of Leclerc] and there was no possibility of completing an overtake from that position".

Objectively, it's hard to disagree with their conclusion up to that point.

The next part of the ruling was more subjective.

"[Leclerc] did not deliberately crowd [Piastri] off the edge of the track. [He] followed the racing line before the entry to Turn Five by moving slightly to the left… and side to side contact occurred between the two cars".

There was an interesting episode in the green room before the podium. This incident played out on the television while Leclerc was sitting watching with Max Verstappen and Kimi Antonelli.

Leclerc's eyes widened and he sucked in a breath as he watched the contact, and he said: "This one was…"

Verstappen interrupted and said: "Was calculated." Leclerc looked at him, they both laughed and then Leclerc sat back looking sheepish and put his cap over his face.

It's clear he recognised the move was on the edge, and a case can be made that he did not comply with the rule that says a driver defending their position by changing line must leave a car's width on the outside if they move back to the racing line.

McLaren team principal Andrea Stella was asked for his view after the race.

He said he had not yet reviewed the stewards' reasoning, but added: "When the cars are staying alongside each other for such a long time, we should make sure that the car that is not on the racing line, because it's defending the inside, does leave enough space, because if there's a contact that is not controlled, then the crash is going to be very big."

I'm sure I read somewhere that this weekend was a record attendance for a Belgian Grand Prix. Given that, and the clear love that drivers and fans share for Spa, it seems ridiculous it won't be on the calendar every year, while far less popular circuits are remaining. Does the FIA care about the views of the fans and drivers or is it all about what races pay the most? - Tom

F1 did say that the attendance at the Belgian Grand Prix this year was an all-time record at 400,000 over the weekend as a whole.

And on a point of fact regarding the question, the calendar is compiled by F1, not the FIA, which merely rubber stamps the schedule at its world council.

In determining which races feature, F1 weighs a number of different factors. Of course, finances are one of them, but money is not everything.

Some races pay smaller fees than others, and one of the reasons for this can be that they are considered to have a greater intrinsic value to the sport.

This would apply to Spa, which has a history and circuit layout that surpasses the vast majority of the rest of the calendar.

However, it is the case that the historic European classic tracks such as Spa and Silverstone - and other events such as Austin - simply cannot afford to pay the same fees as races funded by nation states using them for PR purposes, such as Saudi Arabia and Bahrain, for example.

This is not an easy conundrum to resolve. F1 wants tracks such as Spa on the calendar for all the obvious reasons, but they also have to make financial sense.

It is in trying to find an acceptable compromise to this scenario that the latest situation has arisen, with Spa hosting the Belgian Grand Prix for four out of six years of its current contract.

That means that in 2028 and 2030 Spa will be replaced by another race.

Of course, from the point of view of anyone who appreciates the sport and what it's about, that's not ideal. But it's better than having no Spa at all.

Why are Mercedes struggling to understand George Russell's straight-line deficit? They must have all the data on his battery deployment, ICE output, e-motor power, plus how much drag his aero and car set-up, such as rake angle, generates - Sean

At the Belgian Grand Prix, Mercedes experienced unexplained behaviour from their power unit.

Russell suffered for this, by having a mystery lack of energy over a lap in qualifying. It cost him some but not all of his deficit to team-mate Kimi Antonelli. Mercedes team principal Toto Wolff put the loss from the engine at about 0.2-0.25 seconds.

A pretty much identical thing happened at McLaren to Oscar Piastri in relation to Lando Norris.

Both drivers also experienced a shortfall of energy on the first lap that contributed both to Russell's incident with Hamilton and Antonelli being passed by Max Verstappen on the approach to Eau Rouge.

As Wolff said: "All Mercedes engines had a lack of energy out of Turn One so that wasn't only George. It affected him more than Kimi but also Kimi had that issue to a certain degree."

The one in the race was, Wolff said, "a new issue".

Mercedes said they did not understand why either situation had happened.

The fundamental issue is that the latest F1 hybrid engines are highly complex, as are the rules by which they have to be operated, and the manufacturers and teams are still learning about them.

One of the impacts of this is that small nuances of driving can have a disproportionately large impact on energy availability. It's one of the reasons why the drivers do not like these engines.

McLaren team principal Andrea Stella, among the best technical communicators in the sport, has talked about this extensively this season.

At the weekend in Spa he said: "To operate these power units, you don't operate them in an open-loop way, whereby I would be able to have an off-line simulation and say, 'Oh, that's how the electrical power will be deployed and it will always be identical to itself'.

"There's a large component of the controller that operates the power unit that happens with live calculations and the power unit forward-thinks, forward-calculates what it has to do. There's a lot that happens as the car goes.

"This is why it's not so easy to understand what kind of calculations have been made as the car was going.

"I'm sure we will understand the reason, but at the same time you need to be able to anticipate and make sure that the operation of the power unit unfolds as expected.

"Because this is not only for the influence it has in the power-limited sections, [and] in the straights, but also because it affects the braking points.

"Because if you have an additional amount of harvest before braking, then you are approaching the braking 10km/h slower and your braking point changes.

"This is quite difficult to master for the drivers, and it's one of the reasons why they talk about the difficulties of exploiting the power unit from a driving point of view.

"It's not only to get the most out of the power unit, but also because the variations of the power unit affect the references as you approach a corner.

"But the variations that are significantly relevant are so small that they may happen from one lap to the other, even if you have learned from the previous laps."

This complexity also explains why McLaren have been frustrated this season by some aspects of their relationship with Mercedes, particularly in terms of how much information they have been given about the operation of the engine.

As Stella said: "It's only now, and we are mid-season, that we are starting to have the required tools in order to be able to anticipate, optimise, and study these sensitivities."

Why are drivers allowed to sacrifice their qualifying session to provide a tow to their team-mate? It is understandable during a race, but it feels against the spirit of qualifying - Sylvie

This is a reference to the way Red Bull ran qualifying in Belgium.

Isack Hadjar gave Max Verstappen a tow in the final session that made a difference of about 0.3secs. It allowed Verstappen to qualify on the front row rather than sixth.

If this was to happen routinely, then there might well be consequences.

But this particular station was entirely understandable, and completely normal.

Hadjar had a 30-place grid penalty for exceeding his allowance of power unit components. So, having made it into Q3, he was only ever going to be starting the race at the back, no matter what lap time he set.

In those circumstances, it made complete sense for Red Bull to ask him to give Verstappen a slipstream to maximise his chances.

Antonelli wins to extend lead after Russell crashes out

Antonelli near-perfect but too much drama for Hamilton - driver ratings

Mercedes driver scout Lagrue joining Red Bull

title: 'Tielemans needs a more defensive-minded partner'
description: BBC Radio Manchester's Gaz Drinkwater and Joe McGrath discuss transfers in the latest episode of The Devils' Advocate.  Gaz believes United manager, Michael Carrick, might have some hard decisions to make when it comes to midfield this season.  "I've got a lot of thoughts on our centre-midfield and some concerns," he said on the BBC Sounds podcast.
fullText:
BBC Radio Manchester's Gaz Drinkwater and Joe McGrath discuss transfers in the latest episode of The Devils' Advocate.

Gaz believes United manager, Michael Carrick, might have some hard decisions to make when it comes to midfield this season.

"I've got a lot of thoughts on our centre-midfield and some concerns," he said on the BBC Sounds podcast.

"In an ideal world, Youri Tielemans needs a more defensive-minded partner, which Andrey Santos is. As a centre-midfield pairing, it works quite well.

"But there's just no way United don't play Kobbie Mainoo. Michael Carrick loves him. If Mainoo plays he needs Santos, so a Tielemans and Mainoo partnership doesn't quite fit.

"So it makes me think we should go for another centre-midfielder who would be our marquee signing. Someone like Eduardo Camavinga. But I think that's it."

Listen to the podcast below or on BBC Sounds

title: Hull target Tzolakis is 'good with pressure'
description: Hull City boss Sergej Jakirovic said last week the Tigers were close to completing a deal for Olympiakos goalkeeper Konstantinos Tzolakis.  In light of this, BBC Radio Humberside's James Hoggarth spoke to Greek football journalist Konstantinos Lianos to find out more about Tzolakis.  Every time a Championship club returns to the Premier League, they're automatically dubbed the favourites for relegation.
fullText:
Hull City boss Sergej Jakirovic said last week the Tigers were close to completing a deal for Olympiakos goalkeeper Konstantinos Tzolakis.

In light of this, BBC Radio Humberside's James Hoggarth spoke to Greek football journalist Konstantinos Lianos to find out more about Tzolakis.

"We are talking about a player who is very good with pressure," Lianos explained.

"That's exactly what Hull are about to deal with on their return to the Premier League. Every time a Championship club returns to the Premier League, they're automatically dubbed the favourites for relegation.

"We have historically seen defenders and goalkeepers do better in the Premier League. When it comes to Greek players it's always a safe bet. Tzolakis has played in the Champions League.

"He was the youngest ever Greek goalkeeper to compete in a Greek cup final, aged 17, 10 months and four days. He had the most clean sheets in the 2024-25 Europa League with six clean sheets in 10 games."

title: Why are Premier League transfer fees soaring?
description: With Morgan Rogers set to become the third £100m-plus Premier League transfer of the summer, BBC Sport looks at how the market keeps growing.
fullText:
Thirty-four years ago a £13m transfer caused national outrage in Italy.

When AC Milan signed Gianluigi Lentini from Torino in 1992 for a then world record fee, newspapers called the deal "obscene", while the Vatican's official newspaper described it as "an offence against the dignity of work".

Today that fee would barely register among the Premier League's biggest transfers.

This summer alone Chelsea have agreed a club record £117m deal for Aston Villa's Morgan Rogers, Manchester City paid Nottingham Forest £116m for Elliot Anderson, and Tottenham spent a club record £100m on Sandro Tonali.

These are not the only major deals. Tottenham also agreed an £85m move for West Ham midfielder Mateus Fernandes, Barcelona paid £69m to sign Anthony Gordon from Newcastle United, and Manchester United spent £50m on Chelsea midfielder Andrey Santos.

It is the first transfer window in Premier League history to produce three £100m-plus signings - and there are still weeks to go before deadline day on 1 September.

During the 2025 summer window English top-flight clubs spent a record £3bn.

So why are transfer fees continuing to climb? Why are clubs paying record sums for players already playing in England? And why are more clubs willing to sell some of their biggest stars?

Rogers' move perhaps best encapsulates how today's market works

Chelsea are buying a player from Villa, who have finished above them in three of the past four seasons. Traditionally, clubs might have been reluctant to strengthen a direct rival in those circumstances.

Instead, Chelsea simply met Villa's valuation.

Football finance expert Kieran Maguire says one record fee quickly influences the next.

"It sets a benchmark," he says. "Forest wanted to be close to the record level because they felt Elliot Anderson was a critical player, a player very much in demand.

"We've then seen that surpassed by the Morgan Rogers fee - only slightly - but it allows the club to say, 'This is the record English signing'. So there's an element of bragging rights involved."

After City's £116m move for Anderson established a new market rate, Villa made it clear any club wanting Rogers would need to exceed that figure. Chelsea did exactly that, agreeing a guaranteed £117m fee.

Arsenal, who had identified Rogers as one of their leading targets, explored a deal but refused to meet Villa's asking price.

The episode shows how quickly the market can move. Once a new benchmark is established, comparable players inevitably see their valuations rise.

Part of the answer lies in supply and demand.

Premier League clubs must register at least eight homegrown players in their 25-man squads, while clubs competing in Uefa competitions must also satisfy homegrown quotas. That creates fierce competition for a relatively small pool of players capable of performing consistently at the highest level.

Buying domestically also removes many of the uncertainties associated with overseas recruitment.

Maguire describes Premier League players as "oven-ready".

"Because the Premier League is so wealthy, it's relatively rare that a club is in financial straits where it has to sell," he says.

"In the case of English players, you've then got the homegrown rule, which gives clubs more flexibility.

"You put all of those factors together and it means the Premier League is a big driver."

Clubs know how these players perform against top opposition, and understand they can cope with the physical demands of the Premier League and avoid many of the adaptation risks attached to recruiting from abroad.

That certainty carries a premium. So too does the Premier League's financial strength. Lucrative broadcasting revenues give English clubs spending power unmatched elsewhere in world football, allowing selling clubs to demand higher fees from domestic rivals than they might receive from overseas buyers.

Perhaps the more surprising trend has been clubs allowing key players to leave.

Villa have sold Rogers despite his importance under Unai Emery, while Forest accepted City's offer for Anderson. Newcastle sanctioned Gordon's move despite the England winger becoming one of Eddie Howe's most influential attacking players.

Such decisions reflect commercial strategy as much as football.

Major transfer fees create opportunities to strengthen several areas of a squad rather than relying on one star player, while major sales also provide greater flexibility.

Why are Man City breaking the bank for Anderson?

Those sales have also reignited debate about whether the Premier League's financial regulations place greater pressure on ambitious clubs outside the traditional biggest spenders to sell valuable assets.

Some argue that the rules favour clubs with the greatest financial resources, which are better placed to absorb record transfer fees.

The latest changes are also shaping how clubs approach the transfer market.

Maguire says financial regulations can influence transfer decisions, although he cautions that they are not always the decisive factor.

"Financial regulations might be a driving force, but not necessarily," he says.

"Because clubs don't have to sell from a financial point of view, it means they can extract maximum value from their players."

Villa's sale of Rogers and Forest's decision to cash in on Anderson have become part of that wider discussion, highlighting how regulation now shapes recruitment and squad building across the Premier League.

Rather than viewing these deals simply as losing important players, clubs now see them as opportunities to reshape their squads.

Whether those replacements prove as successful is another matter.

This summer has also brought a significant regulatory change.

The Premier League is transitioning away from profitability and sustainability rules (PSR) towards a new financial framework centred on squad-cost ratio (SCR) and sustainability and systemic resilience (SSR).

Rather than focusing primarily on rolling three-year losses, the new framework places greater emphasis on the relationship between football-related spending and revenue.

Player wages, transfer amortisation – spreading a transfer fee across the length of a player's contract for accounting purposes – and agent fees now become even more central to clubs' financial planning.

For clubs competing in Europe, Uefa's own SCR rules also continue to apply, placing further restrictions on spending.

Why SCR will allow Spurs to keep spending - analysis

Maguire says those accounting rules mean clubs assess transfers very differently from supporters.

"If you're signing a player for £115m, spread over five years that's about £23m a year before wages," he says.

"Getting into the Champions League is worth a fortune and that's the lens through which clubs do those calculations."

Maguire estimates reaching Europe's premier club competition can be worth 180m-200m euros (£153m-£170m) once prize money, matchday revenue and commercial bonuses are taken into account, reinforcing the incentive for clubs to invest heavily in recruitment.

Clubs are already beginning to adapt their transfer strategies. Generating transfer income, reducing wage commitments and carefully balancing spending throughout the year have become key considerations alongside purely footballing decisions.

The Premier League's financial landscape has changed dramatically over the past decade. Since 2016 total summer transfer spending has risen from £1.16bn to a record £3.19bn in 2025 – an increase of almost 175%.

On the surface, that suggests transfer figures are continuing to accelerate. Headline fees, though, tell only part of the story.

Research by Maguire and colleagues at the University of Liverpool suggests some of the Premier League's earlier record signings represented even greater investments when measured against clubs' buying power at the time.

Using Premier League broadcasting revenues as an index, Alan Shearer's £15m move from Blackburn Rovers to Newcastle United in 1996 remains the league's biggest transfer relative to clubs' spending power. Rio Ferdinand's £29.1m move to Manchester United in 2002 and Juan Sebastian Veron's £28.1m arrival at Old Trafford in 2001 also rank above any of this summer's blockbuster deals.

The same model suggests Trevor Francis' £1m move from Birmingham City to Forest in 1979 – English football's first £1m transfer – would equate to about £173m in today's market.

That does not mean the fees paid for Rogers, Anderson and Tonali were modest. Rather, it reflects how dramatically Premier League revenues have grown, allowing clubs to spend sums that would once have been unimaginable.

When Jack Grealish became Britain's first £100m footballer when joining City from Villa in 2021, the transfer marked another turning point in the evolution of the market.

Only five years later, nine-figure deals are no longer viewed as extraordinary. They have become a familiar feature of Premier League transfer windows.

The next milestone may not be far away.

While no Premier League club has yet paid £200m for a player, football's financial ceiling continues to rise and the first transfer of that magnitude no longer feels beyond the realms of possibility.

Will the World Cup affect Arsenal's transfer plans?

title: World Cup final watched by 15.8m people on BBC
description: Spain's victory in Sunday's World Cup final attracts a peak audience of 15.8 million on BBC One and BBC iPlayer.
fullText:
Spain's victory over Argentina in Sunday's World Cup final attracted a peak audience of 15.8 million on BBC One and BBC iPlayer.

The BBC enjoyed a 64% share of all TV viewing for the match, with an average of 13.9 million watching as Ferran Torres' extra-time strike sealed Spain's second World Cup triumph.

The final was streamed 11.1 million times on BBC iPlayer and the BBC Sport website and app, including 2.4m UHD streams on iPlayer.

The BBC Sport live text coverage on the website and app was viewed more than 18.8 million times, including 14.2 million views in the UK, providing fans with the latest news, analysis and updates from the match.

Saturday's third-place play-off between England and France brought in a peak audience of 11.8 million on BBC One and iPlayer, with an average audience of 10.8 million.

Across the World Cup as a whole, the BBC saw an overall reach of 42.8 million across TV viewing.

Audiences streamed World Cup matches 237 million times on BBC iPlayer and the BBC Sport website and app, more than double the 2022 World Cup, and football dominated BBC iPlayer, with World Cup content accounting for 33% of everything streamed during the competition.

Additionally, the BBC Sport website and app attracted 57.2 million unique visitors, with fans viewing BBC Sport content 1.7 billion times, match highlights of World Cup matches were streamed 67.3 million times while the new BBC Football YouTube channel generated 100 million views across a diverse range of content.

Across social media, clips, interviews, analysis, press conferences and fan moments attracted 2.55 billion video views.

The top-performing video of the World Cup was Egypt winger Mostafa Zico's interview after Egypt went out to Argentina, with 77 million views on TikTok alone.

Alex Kay-Jelski, Director of BBC Sport, said: "This has been a World Cup we'll never forget, and that's entirely down to the millions of people who chose to experience it with the BBC.

"Thank you to everyone who watched, streamed, listened, read, shared and joined us throughout the tournament.

"Whether you were watching at home, following on your phone during the working day, catching up on Football Daily or diving into our new interactive experiences, you made this a summer of football that brought the nation together."

24m tune in to watch England-Argentina

How to follow the new football season on BBC Sport

title: What did England learn during ODI series win over India?
description: After beating India 2-1, BBC Sport asks if England are finally building momentum one year out from the World Cup.
fullText:
What a difference three weeks makes.

At the end of June, a dark cloud hung over the England camp, with Test captain Ben Stokes retiring after a 2-1 series defeat by New Zealand.

That campaign brought an end to coach Brendon McCullum's time in charge of the red-ball side, and plenty of questions remain about the future of the Test team, but the New Zealander has enjoyed an upturn in fortunes in July, with his limited-overs outfit beating India 4-0 in a T20 series and 2-1 in one-day internationals.

Defeating the world number one side in both formats, and overtaking them at the top of the rankings in the 20-over game, is no mean feat and England may feel they are now beginning to show the sort of momentum they last displayed in the build-up to their World Cup victory in 2019.

With the next edition of the tournament a little over a year away, BBC Sport takes a look at whether England's 50-over team is finally tracking in the right direction.

England's only significant change in the India series was a new opening combination of Ben Duckett and Jacob Bethell, and it bore fruit at Lord's.

The left-handed duo became England's sixth opening pair since the 2023 World Cup, with Phil Salt, Jamie Smith and Zak Crawley currently out of contention and Will Jacks and Rehan Ahmed considerations for the middle order after spells at the top.

They had a decent start in the first ODI at Edgbaston, putting on 63, but Duckett was dismissed first ball in the second fixture at Cardiff, with Bethell following shortly afterwards as England found themselves 8-2.

In London, however, the pair flogged a tired India attack, taking advantage of benign batting conditions to put on the highest ODI opening stand at Lord's, with Duckett pressing on for his fourth century in the format.

Bethell, who has not previously batted higher than four for England in white-ball cricket, started his ODI opening career with innings of 14 and four, but cut a composed figure at the Home of Cricket.

He played in a typically positive fashion, striking at 97.85 as he plundered 11 fours and two sixes before falling for 91, just short of a second ODI ton.

The 22-year-old's promotion up the order means England can carry an extra bowler, with the hosts fielding three seamers and spinner Adil Rashid alongside all-rounders Sam Curran and Will Jacks in the latter two matches of this series.

If England are to regain the World Cup, having surrendered it meekly in 2023, much of the responsibility will rest on Joe Root's shoulders.

The 35-year-old struggled by his standards in the one-day format for a number of years following the 2019 victory, averaging just 29 between 2020 and 2023, and his record saw him left out of the ODI set-up for the entirety of 2024.

However since his recall, Root has enjoyed a remarkable uptick in form, averaging 81.5 from 21 innings with four centuries.

He has now made six consecutive scores over 50, stretching back to the start of the series in Sri Lanka in January, becoming the first England batter to achieve that feat.

Root's unbeaten 76 at Edgbaston kept England in contention, with a 121-run partnership with Liam Dawson coming between collapses of 6-107 and 4-30, while an undefeated 99 in Cardiff anchored England's successful chase.

Coming in after the record-breaking opening stand on Sunday, Root enjoyed a rare opportunity to cut loose, with his strike-rate of 154.17 the highest of any ODI score over 50 in his ODI career.

If there is a question to be answered about England's side, it is who will fill the seamer slots in the XI.

With the World Cup to be played on the hard decks of South Africa and Zimbabwe, England will likely persist with three outright fast bowlers with the support of Curran.

Spearhead Jofra Archer is an automatic selection, assuming he remains fit, while Gus Atkinson did his cause no harm in his two outings against India, taking 3-50 in Cardiff and keeping to an economy rate of four at Lord's when no other bowler was under six.

The third slot remains up for grabs, with neither Josh Tongue nor Saqib Mahmood consolidating their place in the side.

Tongue took one wicket in two matches and was expensive at Lord's, conceding 43 runs from five overs, while Mahmood took 2-52 in Cardiff but was rotated out for Tongue for the series finale.

Brydon Carse returned to the squad for this series having recovered from wrist and elbow injuries that have kept him out of international cricket, while the selectors have also favoured the all-round skills of Jamie Overton and may return to him once he has recovered from the thigh injury he sustained in the Indian Premier League.

With a five-match Ashes series preceding the World Cup, England may expect at least one seamer to go down with injury, and will want a strong group of pacers ready to step up to the tournament squad.

There is also slight concern about spinner Adil Rashid, who has had a quiet 2026, taking eight wickets in ODIs and only one in this series.

The 38-year-old's economy rate for the year is consistent with recent efforts, remaining under six, while he can still claim to be England's biggest threat in overs 11-40 - taking 32 of their 72 wickets in that period since the start of last year.

England will return to the one-day format at the tail-end of the summer against Sri Lanka, with matches at Chester-le-Street, Headingley and The Oval between 22-27 September.

Their next taste of international cricket will be in the format as they play three matches in Australia in mid-November, before they sample World Cup conditions with three fixtures in South Africa in mid-January.

There is set to be an ODI series against Pakistan in the 2027 summer before the World Cup starts on 4 October.

England learning on the job in ODIs - Root

England seal series with record Lord's ODI total

title: Thank you for making this a World Cup to remember on the BBC
description: BBC Director of Sport Alex Kay-Jelski's message as BBC Sport delivers a record-breaking World Cup across TV, radio, online and social.
fullText:
As an incredible World Cup comes to an end, everyone here at the BBC wanted to say thank you.

Thank you to the 42.8 million people who tuned in to our live match coverage on television or streamed games on iPlayer, the BBC Sport app and website 237 million times. That's a lot of football.

Whether you were one of the 24 million who watched England v Argentina in the semi-final, the late-night 9.1 million who stayed up until 4am for England v Mexico, the 2.4 million who saw Scotland beat Haiti in the middle of the night, or part of the hardcore crew of half a million who gave Iran v New Zealand a chance at 2am and were rewarded with a four-goal thriller.

Thank you to those of you who trusted us to not spoil your mornings, coming to iPlayer or the BBC Football YouTube channel every day for our eight-minute highlight packages. There were so many of you that we saw the biggest ever day and week in iPlayer's history.

Thank you to the 57.2 million unique browsers who used our app and website 1.7 billion times, watching clips of our games, interviews and journalism, following our amazing live pages and reading stories from our journalists covering everything from the Trump-Fifa-Balogun scandal to tactical trends and the US entry denial of Somalian referee Omar Artan.

World Cup final watched by 15.8m people on BBC

And to those of you who spent ages deep in our new 3D tool, watching the game as if you were Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo or Erling Haaland charging down the goalkeeper - it was used almost five million times. We love trying new things and you using them helps us decide what to do next.

Thank you to the millions of you on the move tuning in to the games on Radio 5 Live or BBC Sounds, realising there are few better ways to sit on the edge of your seat than listening to John Murray, Ian Dennis and the team. And to those who helped the visualised Football Daily podcast reach more people than ever before.

As for those of you whose fingers are tired from six weeks of swiping more than two billion times through our social and digital content, thank you for devouring our behind-the-scenes footage of Harry Kane's hilarious high-pitch interview, our attempts to introduce Olivier Giroud to Irn-Bru, our YouTube tactical dissections and, of course, Wayne Rooney, Joe Hart and Micah Richards' rowing trip down the Hudson.

Thank you to all the new people using the BBC, buying licence fees from QR codes or finding our app, socials or shows for the first time. It's great to have you with us.

There's lots more we have to offer if you stick around too, whether that is downloading our app with its swipeable video feed, following your favourite sport or team for a personalised experience, or checking out our range of documentaries and podcasts across iPlayer and Sounds.

It doesn't have to be sport either - there are amazing dramas, comedies, music, children's shows and news coverage across the BBC, to mention just a few things.

Mostly, however, you spent time with the BBC during the World Cup - and 94% of you in the country did - thank you for choosing us and trusting us.

We loved bringing all the action, fun and drama to you.

How to follow the new football season on BBC Sport

Follow your club with BBC Sport

Notifications, social media and more with BBC Sport

title: Why are Premier League transfer fees soaring?
description: With Morgan Rogers set to become the third £100m-plus Premier League transfer of the summer, BBC Sport looks at how the market keeps growing.
fullText:
Thirty-four years ago a £13m transfer caused national outrage in Italy.

When AC Milan signed Gianluigi Lentini from Torino in 1992 for a then world record fee, newspapers called the deal "obscene", while the Vatican's official newspaper described it as "an offence against the dignity of work".

Today that fee would barely register among the Premier League's biggest transfers.

This summer alone Chelsea have agreed a club record £117m deal for Aston Villa's Morgan Rogers, Manchester City paid Nottingham Forest £116m for Elliot Anderson, and Tottenham spent a club record £100m on Sandro Tonali.

These are not the only major deals. Tottenham also agreed an £85m move for West Ham midfielder Mateus Fernandes, Barcelona paid £69m to sign Anthony Gordon from Newcastle United, and Manchester United spent £50m on Chelsea midfielder Andrey Santos.

It is the first transfer window in Premier League history to produce three £100m-plus signings - and there are still weeks to go before deadline day on 1 September.

During the 2025 summer window English top-flight clubs spent a record £3bn.

So why are transfer fees continuing to climb? Why are clubs paying record sums for players already playing in England? And why are more clubs willing to sell some of their biggest stars?

Rogers' move perhaps best illustrates how today's market works.

Chelsea are buying a player from Villa, who have finished above them in three of the past four seasons. Traditionally, clubs might have been reluctant to strengthen a direct rival in those circumstances.

Instead, Chelsea simply met Villa's valuation.

Football finance expert Kieran Maguire says one record fee quickly influences the next.

"It sets a benchmark," he says. "Forest wanted to be close to the record level because they felt Elliot Anderson was a critical player, a player very much in demand.

"We've then seen that surpassed by the Morgan Rogers fee - only slightly - but it allows the club to say, 'This is the record English signing'. So there's an element of bragging rights involved."

After City's £116m move for Anderson established a new market rate, Villa made it clear any club wanting Rogers would need to exceed that figure. Chelsea did exactly that, agreeing a guaranteed £117m fee.

Arsenal, who had identified Rogers as one of their leading targets, explored a deal but refused to meet Villa's asking price.

The episode illustrates how quickly the market can move. Once a new benchmark is established, comparable players inevitably see their valuations rise.

Part of the answer lies in supply and demand.

Premier League clubs must register at least eight homegrown players in their 25-man squads, while clubs competing in Uefa competitions must also satisfy homegrown quotas. That creates fierce competition for a relatively small pool of players capable of performing consistently at the highest level.

Buying domestically also removes many of the uncertainties associated with overseas recruitment.

Maguire describes Premier League players as "oven-ready".

"Because the Premier League is so wealthy, it's relatively rare that a club is in financial straits where it has to sell," he says.

"In the case of English players, you've then got the homegrown rule, which gives clubs more flexibility.

"You put all of those factors together and it means the Premier League is a big driver."

Clubs know how these players perform against top opposition, and understand they can cope with the physical demands of the Premier League and avoid many of the adaptation risks attached to recruiting from abroad.

That certainty carries a premium. So too does the Premier League's financial strength. Lucrative broadcasting revenues give English clubs spending power unmatched elsewhere in world football, allowing selling clubs to demand higher fees from domestic rivals than they might receive from overseas buyers.

Perhaps the more surprising trend has been clubs allowing key players to leave.

Villa have sold Rogers despite his importance under Unai Emery, while Forest accepted City's offer for Anderson. Newcastle sanctioned Gordon's move despite the England winger becoming one of Eddie Howe's most influential attacking players.

Such decisions reflect commercial strategy as much as football.

Major transfer fees create opportunities to strengthen several areas of a squad rather than relying on one star player, while major sales also provide greater flexibility.

Why are Man City breaking the bank for Anderson?

Those sales have also reignited debate about whether the Premier League's financial regulations place greater pressure on ambitious clubs outside the traditional biggest spenders to sell valuable assets.

Some argue that the rules favour clubs with the greatest financial resources, which are better placed to absorb record transfer fees.

The latest changes are also shaping how clubs approach the transfer market.

Maguire says financial regulations can influence transfer decisions, although he cautions that they are not always the decisive factor.

"Financial regulations might be a driving force, but not necessarily," he says.

"Because clubs don't have to sell from a financial point of view, it means they can extract maximum value from their players."

Villa's sale of Rogers and Forest's decision to cash in on Anderson have become part of that wider discussion, illustrating how regulation now shapes recruitment and squad building across the Premier League.

Rather than viewing these deals simply as losing important players, clubs now see them as opportunities to reshape their squads.

Whether those replacements prove as successful is another matter.

This summer has also brought a significant regulatory change.

The Premier League is transitioning away from profitability and sustainability rules (PSR) towards a new financial framework centred on squad-cost ratio (SCR) and sustainability and systemic resilience (SSR).

Rather than focusing primarily on rolling three-year losses, the new framework places greater emphasis on the relationship between football-related spending and revenue.

Player wages, transfer amortisation – spreading a transfer fee across the length of a player's contract for accounting purposes – and agent fees now become even more central to clubs' financial planning.

For clubs competing in Europe, Uefa's own SCR rules also continue to apply, placing further restrictions on spending.

Why SCR will allow Spurs to keep spending - analysis

Maguire says those accounting rules mean clubs assess transfers very differently from supporters.

"If you're signing a player for £115m, spread over five years that's about £23m a year before wages," he says.

"Getting into the Champions League is worth a fortune and that's the lens through which clubs do those calculations."

Maguire estimates reaching Europe's premier club competition can be worth 180m-200m euros (£153m-£170m) once prize money, matchday revenue and commercial bonuses are taken into account, reinforcing the incentive for clubs to invest heavily in recruitment.

Clubs are already beginning to adapt their transfer strategies. Generating transfer income, reducing wage commitments and carefully balancing spending throughout the year have become key considerations alongside purely footballing decisions.

The Premier League's financial landscape has changed dramatically over the past decade. Since 2016 total summer transfer spending has risen from £1.16bn to a record £3.19bn in 2025 – an increase of almost 175%.

On the surface, that suggests transfer figures are continuing to accelerate. Headline fees, though, tell only part of the story.

Research by Maguire and colleagues at the University of Liverpool suggests some of the Premier League's earlier record signings represented even greater investments when measured against clubs' buying power at the time.

Using Premier League broadcasting revenues as an index, Alan Shearer's £15m move from Blackburn Rovers to Newcastle United in 1996 remains the league's biggest transfer relative to clubs' spending power. Rio Ferdinand's £29.1m move to Manchester United in 2002 and Juan Sebastian Veron's £28.1m arrival at Old Trafford in 2001 also rank above any of this summer's blockbuster deals.

The same model suggests Trevor Francis' £1m move from Birmingham City to Forest in 1979 – English football's first £1m transfer – would equate to about £173m in today's market.

That does not mean the fees paid for Rogers, Anderson and Tonali were modest. Rather, it illustrates how dramatically Premier League revenues have grown, allowing clubs to spend sums that would once have been unimaginable.

When Jack Grealish became Britain's first £100m footballer when joining City from Villa in 2021, the transfer marked another turning point in the evolution of the market.

Only five years later, nine-figure deals are no longer viewed as extraordinary. They have become a familiar feature of Premier League transfer windows.

The next milestone may not be far away.

While no Premier League club has yet paid £200m for a player, football's financial ceiling continues to rise and the first transfer of that magnitude no longer feels beyond the realms of possibility.

Will the World Cup affect Arsenal's transfer plans?

title: 'Medal guarantee' Wiffen ready for Commonwealth Games
description: Daniel Wiffen tells BBC Sport NI about his breakthrough at the 2022 Commonwealth Games, dealing with his newfound fame and his unwavering self-belief.
fullText:
He has won gold in the Olympics, Worlds and Europeans since taking silver in the 2022 Commonwealth Games, but Daniel Wiffen says that second-place finish in Birmingham still "sits over my head".

Ultra-competitive by nature, Wiffen acknowledges that the Games four years ago were the springboard to the unprecedented success that has since followed, including 800m gold and 1500m bronze at the Paris Olympics in 2024.

That is why, two years out from the 2028 Olympics in Los Angeles, he is looking forward to this year's Commonwealth Games in Glasgow, where he will compete in the 400m, 800m and 1500m and aim to add more gold medals to his burgeoning collection.

"I think after Tokyo [Olympics in 2021] we had a three-year plan and the first was to medal at the Commonwealth Games. You get the first thing ticked off the list and you're on the way. It was the catalyst which started my swimming career," Wiffen told BBC Sport NI.

"The Commonwealth Games silver sits over my head as I wish I won it, but I've always said in 2026 I want to win every competition.

"I had a bit of a drought before the Europeans and I wasn't in my top form for like a year and I think Commonwealth Games, I'm looking forward to getting my redemption and getting a gold medal - at least one - and to be honest, I want three but I have to see."

Life has changed drastically for Wiffen since Birmingham four years ago as a result of his success in the pool.

He is now regularly stopped in the street for photographs and has enjoyed his newfound fame.

"I like it, it's cool people recognise the hard work you put in. I've been swimming since I was seven and my parents always said they never thought I'd become a celebrity in Ireland. You get a lot of free stuff which they like!" he joked.

Wiffen also gives an insight into life as a high-performing athlete by regularly posting YouTube videos on a channel he shares with his twin brother Nathan.

The channel has gained over 100,000 subscribers and the 24-year-old believes the experience of posting videos in which he talks to a camera has helped him with media duties as a swimmer.

"For Paris I was posting videos all the way along and people were like you should stop and hide it but I was like, to be honest, I'm training faster than anyone else and I'm making them scared," he said.

"People find media interviews daunting but I think because I started my YouTube channel when I was 18 and I've been doing that, I've become quite good on camera and quite good at being myself instead of putting on a fake persona that other people do."

Wiffen is part of Team NI's 15-strong swimming squad heading to Glasgow and is one of their genuine multiple medal contenders, although he would rather refer to himself as a "medal guarantee".

Bethany Firth, Barry McClements and Wiffen brought home NI's three medals in Birmingham, but the Magheralin man believes more medals will follow this year given the work that has gone into developing the swimming programme across the island of Ireland.

"One of the main things you notice over the years is the systems building up the athletes have got more experience from outside resources and people from different countries who have had success before and they can build the athletes up from a younger age.

"No doubt we'll have a better medal count than in Birmingham, I think we'll have multiple golds."

Wiffen's self-belief is to be admired and has only been strengthened by a tough spell in 2025 in which his form dipped as he battled back from appendicitis surgery.

He recovered to win three medals at the European short course championships, which gives him the confidence that he can bounce back from future setbacks in whatever form they take.

"Before European short course last year I had a bit of doubt and that's the first time in my life, it was because I had appendicitis and I had a month to train, from zero to 100 in a month to win the Europeans and after I did that I believe I can win anything.

"After that setback nothing is going to stop me."

title: What has changed in The Hundred for 2026?
description: BBC Sport's Ask Me Anything team looks at what has changed for this year's edition of The Hundred.
fullText:
The 2026 edition of The Hundred gets under way on Tuesday and will have some significant changes from previous years.

In 2025, the ECB finalised the sale of stakes in all eight teams with over £500m of the funds generated set to be reinvested back into English and Welsh cricket.

As part of the changes, some team names and badges have also been updated to reflect their new owners.

Here is a look at all of the changes coming on and off the field for this year's competition.

Since the 2025 Hundred campaign ended last August, all eight teams have been either partially or fully taken over by new owners as part of significant financial investment into the competition.

Of those eight, four have gone to ownership groups that are also involved in the Indian Premier League (IPL) franchises.

Chelsea co-owner Todd Boehly is also among the new investors after purchasing a 49% stake in the Trent Rockets.

Following the influx of new ownership, there have also been some changes made to a few team news, badges and kits.

Three team names have changed to be in line with IPL clubs they are partners with. The Northern Superchargers have become Sunrisers Leeds, the Oval Invincibles are now known as MI London and the Manchester Originals have become the Manchester Super Giants.

That trio of teams have also updated their badges and kits following their re-branding.

While the Southern Brave have not changed their name, their colour scheme has switched from green and black to blue and red.

MI London have also switched colour schemes to blue and gold, in line with their partner IPL side the Mumbai Indians.

London Spirit have changed jerseys from dark blue to purple and black with a red and yellow trim.

Sunrisers Leeds have swapped from purple and white strips to orange and black.

Manchester Super Giants are yet to release their shirt for the campaign, but have changed their badge to a red, white and yellow colour scheme with an elephant on it.

Trent Rockets, Welsh Fire and Birmingham Phoenix are the only teams to not make significant changes to their badges or shirt colour schemes.

Grace Ballinger column: How will a revamped Hundred play out?

The opening match of the 2026 edition of The Hundred is in the women's competition between MI London and Sunrisers Leeds at The Oval.

That starts at 14:45 BST on Tuesday, 21 July. The two teams will meet later that evening in the men's competition.

There are daily matches from then until Wednesday, 12 August, which is the final day of the group stage in both competitions.

On Friday, 14 August the eliminator will take place in both competitions. There, the teams finishing second and third in the group stage will meet in a play-off, with the winner facing the group stage winners in the final.

The finals for both competitions take place two days later on Sunday, 16 August in a double header at Lord's.

New investment brought major changes in the player auction, which took place in March.

In both competitions, teams were allowed to pre- England white ball captain Harry Brook was the most expensive signing, staying at Sunrisers Leeds for £460,000.

England white ball batter Phil Salt signed for Welsh Fire in a £450,000 move, while England fast bowler Jofra Archer remains at Southern Brave, having been picked up for £400,000.

Archer is joined by England's Test wicketkeeper Jamie Smith, joining the Brave for £300,000.

English all-rounder James Coles broke the record for most expensive Hundred signing from the auction when joining London Spirit for £390,000. England wicket-keeper Jordan Cox also signed for Welsh Fire for £300,000.

Former England Test captain Joe Root joined Welsh Fire for £240,000, while England white-ball spinner Adil Rashid was acquired for £250,000 by Southern Brave.

England pace bowlers Josh Tongue and Gus Atkinson both signed for the Manchester Super Giants for £200,000 and £70,000 respectively.

In the women's competition, England captain Nat Sciver-Brunt and Lauren Bell were the joint-most expensive retention signings at £140,000 each. All-rounder Sciver-Brunt is staying with the Trent Rockets, with Lauren Bell staying at Southern Brave.

England all-rounder Alice Capsey has joined Birmingham Phoenix, having been sold for £130,000 in the retentions round.

Following a one-year break from the competition, India batter Smriti Mandhana has joined Manchester Super Giants for £90,000. England spinner Sophie Ecclestone is alongside, being retained at £110,000.

New Zealand all-rounder Sophie Devine and Australia wicket-keeper Beth Mooney both recorded the most expensive women's signings. The duo, both sold for £210,000, joined the Welsh Fire and Trent Rockets respectively.

England fast bowler Issy Wong has signed for Southern Brave for £130,000. Wong joins England spinners Tilly Corteen-Coleman and Sarah Glenn, who the Brave signed for £105,000 and £75,000 respectively.

Batter Tammy Beaumont, who recently called time on her international career, was signed by Birmingham Phoenix for £70,000.

2025 - MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

2024 - MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

2023 - MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

2025 – Sunrisers Leeds (formerly Northern Superchargers)

2022 – MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

2021 – MI London (formerly Oval Invincibles)

You will be able to follow the tournament across the BBC, including 16 matches live on BBC Two and BBC iPlayer.

Isa Guha, Alex Hartley and Amber Sandhu will present the coverage with Michael Vaughan, Sir Alastair Cook and Phil Tufnell returning as pundits.

New faces include England Test opener Emilio Gay and seamer Mark Wood, while former India batter Ajinkya Rahane and New Zealand wicketkeeper Katey Martin will also be involved.

World Cup-winners Tymal Mills, Ebony Rainford-Brent, Dawid Malan, Amanda Jade-Wellington and Carlos Brathwaite will also be part of the coverage.

There will be ball-by-ball audio commentary on all 68 matches on BBC Sounds, with extensive coverage on BBC Radio 5 Live and 5 Sports Extra.

The BBC Sport website and app will have reports on every game and daily video highlights of the best moments, while there will be live text commentary and in-play video clips on the Eliminator and finals.

On social media, the BBC Cricket Instagram account will also have all the best moments, plus you can keep up to date with the competition via the BBC Cricket WhatsApp channel.

BBC Sport's YouTube channel will have highlights throughout the tournament, and keep an eye out for 'Ravi meets' - a new feature with a partially-sighted cricket fan who took BBC Sport's social channels by storm in the summer of 2025.

Times are BST and subject to change (women's game first, then men's)

Tuesday, 21 July: MI London v Sunrisers Leeds (14:45 & 18:30)

Wednesday, 22 July: Southern Brave v Welsh Fire (14:45 & 18:30)

Sunday, 26 July: Trent Rockets v London Spirit (14:30 & 18:00)

Sunday, 2 August: MI London v Manchester Super Giants (14:30 & 18:00)

Tuesday, 4 August: Sunrisers Leeds v London Spirit (15:00 & 18:30)

Wednesday, 5 August: Trent Rockets v Birmingham Phoenix (15:00 & 18:30)

Sunday, 9 August: London Spirit v Birmingham Phoenix (14:30 & 18:00)

Sunday, 16 August: Finals (14:15 & 18:00)
