title: Best goal, best player, best fans - BBC reporters' World Cup awards
description: BBC Sport reporters pick their best moments of the tournament - and you can vote for yours.
fullText:
After delivering unforgettable drama and surprises across 104 matches over almost six weeks, the World Cup is over.

History has been scripted, records broken and heroes old and new made their mark at football's most coveted tournament.

Read our reporters' reflections from the US, Canada and Mexico and then choose from our chief football writer Phil McNulty's selections for best goal and best save.

Spain captain Rodri won the official World Cup Golden Ball trophy as the tournament's best player.

Messi won the Silver Ball as the second-best player, with Mbappe named Bronze Ball winner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Surprise, surprise. Lionel Messi. Not simply a swansong for a 39-year-old closing down his career, but the enduring inspiration of a nation and a football team. Look at how he controlled the game and pulled the strings when Argentina chased England down in a dramatic semi-final.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

I was in a Kylian Mbappe news conference early in the World Cup and I remember thinking that I'd never seen him looking more relaxed, more confident and more determined to make this his tournament. That demeanour off the pitch was reflected in some brilliant performances on the pitch - great goals, leadership, flair, composure under pressure and more work off the ball than I've ever seen from him.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The player of the tournament has been Jude Bellingham. The Real Madrid midfielder dragged England through by sheer force of will in some games and took his game to another level during the tournament.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Lionel Messi's leadership, football intelligence and ability to decide the biggest matches has proven vital for Argentina. He was the driving force behind their run to the final and that makes him the competition's standout player.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

With eight goals and four assists in eight games at this World Cup, it has to be Lionel Messi. England did their best to keep him quiet in the semi-final and succeeded in the first half, but Messi often finds a way and his two assists proved decisive.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Michael Olise. Marked out of the semi-final against Spain but the former Crystal Palace forward, who started his journey aged six at Hayes & Yeading United, has left his mark on this tournament with his seven assists. At just 24, Olise has another World Cup or two up his sleeve. I'm still playing replays in my mind of that spectacular scissor-kick against Sweden in the last 32. The whole press box erupted thinking it was going in, only for the ball to crash against a post.

Rodri. He has proved to be irreplaceable for his country as he is for Manchester City. Exceptional and so effective at the heart of the best team in the tournament

Easy choice but it has to be Lionel Messi. Doing this at the age of 39 is almost defying the law of physics. He is cementing his place as the greatest.

Spain's 19-year-old centre-back Pau Cubarsi received the Fifa award for the tournament's best young player after playing every minute of their World Cup campaign.

He was a key figure in the Spain defence that conceded only one goal all tournament.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

I am going for Spain's 19-year-old central defender Pau Cubarsi. The Barcelona youngster was magnificent against France in the semi-final and is a star of the future.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Lille fans, regular viewers of Ligue 1 and recruitment teams from the top clubs knew all about his potential but Morocco's Ayyoub Bouaddi burst on to the world stage in style with an exceptional performance in their opening match against Brazil. At the New York New Jersey Stadium that day, I found myself not watching the ball but watching his incredibly intelligent movement around the pitch, which reminded me of a cross between Patrick Vieira and Luka Modric.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

It sounds strange to call Erling Haaland a breakout star but it is hard to explain the impact he has had here in the US. He has become a household name and even more of a global superstar.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cape Verde's 40-year-old goalkeeper Vozinha. He began his unforgettable World Cup with seven sensational saves in a 0–0 draw against Spain in their debut World Cup match and ended it with eight more remarkable saves as Cape Verde pushed Lionel Messi and Argentina to the brink of elimination. Vozinha's heroics sparked a global following, with his Instagram audience skyrocketing from about 50,000 before the tournament to more than 29 million.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

It seemed like the USA would go far at this tournament, particularly with the wave of support behind them, but it wasn't to be as they exited at the last-16 stage. Despite that, many of their players did their own reputations no harm and one of those was full-back Alex Freeman, with his performances leading to USA boss Mauricio Pochettino saying he could be one of the best in the world in his position.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Step forward Switzerland's Johan Manzambi. Some 20-year-olds might be overwhelmed playing on the biggest stage of all. Not Manzambi. This huge talent plays with a smile on his face and is a natural entertainer who departed the tournament with three goals in four games.

Johan Manzambi. I highlighted him before the World Cup and, although he missed the end for Switzerland through injury, the fact he is moving to the Premier League with Aston Villa for a fee in the region of £50m underlines his quality.

The 17-year-old Mexico midfielder Gilberto Mora, who ended up in their starting line-up. Had the air of Luka Modric and seems destined for the top of the game.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The Azteca for Mexico v England. Might just be the best atmosphere I have ever experienced in seven World Cups. It was an assault on the senses and the singing of the Mexican national anthem was a spine-tingling experience. A simply unforgettable night.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Speaking to the Paraguay fans outside the Boston stadium, moments after one of the most dramatic penalty shootouts I have ever seen, as the South American side stunned Germany. One father and son, draped in Paraguay flags, were in tears as they told me that this was the best day of their lives, and it summed up what special moments at a World Cup can mean to people. I will never forget the emotion of those fans streaming out of the ground in disbelief.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Lionel Messi scoring a hat-trick in his first game at this World Cup showed that he was not done and was going to try to push Argentina to another tournament win.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Sidny Cabral's stunning 103rd-minute equaliser against Argentina. His strike from the edge of the penalty area that flew beyond Emiliano Martinez sent the stadium into disbelief and brought Cape Verde level at 2-2 in extra time. Although Argentina eventually edged the tie 3-2, Cabral's wonder goal became an instant classic and will always be talked about.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

For me it was Argentina being pushed by Cape Verde all the way before the debutants were knocked out. They showed the sort of guts and desire we want to see from these teams that gained the opportunity to play at a World Cup because of the expanded format.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

On my travels around the US, where I have covered 13 games in six different cities, I have spoken to dozens of fans. I was offered a beer and sausages cooked from the back of a pick-up truck before France-Paraguay in Philadelphia, and been stopped for selfies. "You're from the BBC? I have to have a selfie with the guy from the BBC." I have lost count of the number of times I have stopped a fan and introduced myself, only to be asked: "Is it coming home?"

For the moment I actually witnessed, it would have to be Sidny Cabral and his sensational equaliser against Argentina as Cape Verde, the smallest nation to ever reach the knockout stages, stunned the defending champions for a second time to make it 2-2 in Miami.

Cabral's astonishing goal against Argentina. It was as funny as it was breathtaking.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Brazil. Slow. Uninspired. Un-Brazilian. Trudged through the World Cup under Carlo Ancelotti and every weakness was exposed when they deservedly went out to Norway in the last 16.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

Senegal came to the tournament with the most talented squad in their history but various problems off the pitch arguably meant the players were not given the right environment to thrive. When they were 2-0 up in the last-32 match against Belgium, it looked as though they would salvage something from this World Cup, but tactical errors and poor game management cost them. It was a massive wasted opportunity for a gifted generation of Senegal players.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

Scotland missing out on third-placed qualification was a disappointment and having to wait a couple of days before it was confirmed must have been painful.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Cristiano Ronaldo and Portugal. As one of the pre-tournament favourites, they fell short after a 1–0 last-16 defeat by Spain. Ronaldo managed three goals in five matches and, despite high expectations, Portugal never reached the level many expected, ending another World Cup campaign in frustrating fashion.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

The USA. They started this tournament so well and created a real wave of optimism and hope among the home fans, but all that was undone by the controversy surrounding Folarin Balogun and then their limp exit at the hands of Belgium.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

The final being held at the MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. Yes, it is huge but it is not fan-friendly and it is an expensive pain to get to and get away from. Philadelphia would have been my pick. A stadium on the outskirts of the city in a sports complex. The MetLife is a giant bowl in the middle of nowhere.

France in their semi-final defeat by Spain. I tipped Spain to beat France but still did not expect such a flat performance and their star-studded frontline to flounder as badly as they did.

I really thought Brazil under Ancelotti would go further. And clearly so did they.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Mexico. The sheer passion and love for their team and football was incredible. Also such friendly, welcoming fans and people. Incredibly warm and gracious in defeat.

We were in a restaurant in Mexico City the night before they played England, and an MC went around each table asking which country people were from. When we told them we were from England, it was announced to the rest of the diners, who burst into a spontaneous outbreak of applause.

John Bennett, BBC World Service

A word for the Curacao fans I was with inside the Philadelphia Stadium - because who knew that supporters from a nation of just 150,000 could make that much noise? Was there anyone left on the island? But the Brazil fans win it for me because they do not just take over stadiums; they take over cities; Philadelphia felt like Rio or Sao Paulo for 48 hours when they were in town.

Alex Howell, BBC Sport's England reporter

The Colombia fans took over Arrowhead Stadium in Kansas City when they played Ghana, and made it feel like a party.

Liz Conway, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's fans were brilliant, with their legendary Viking row taking over the World Cup. The chant became a global sensation and created an unforgettable atmosphere in every stadium they entered. The most iconic moments came after matches when the players joined the fans to lead the Viking row from the pitch, uniting thousands of supporters in celebration.

Gary Rose, BBC Sport journalist

I was based mainly in Los Angeles for the World Cup. There's a large Mexican population there and from what I experienced, they were undoubtedly the best supporters. They were loud - very loud - for every Mexico game and when they won fireworks could be heard around LA while their distinct green jerseys were seen everywhere.

Neil Johnston, BBC Sport journalist

Norway's Viking row was fun to watch, with fans wearing red shirts sitting in lines and rowing in rhythm. The Norway supporters also provided an amusing moment during the quarter-final with England. They sang "You're going home, you're going home, England's going home" as they led 1-0 in Miami. Moments later Jude Bellingham equalised.

The Mexicans. I was fortunate to experience two matches in the Azteca. That is where the heart of this World Cup was and the support for their team was a joy to behold.

Argentina. On two occasions in Miami and Kansas City, I have been in stadiums where their support has been in excess of 60,000 fans. The sheer scale of numbers is matched by their wild passion. They sing constantly and have generated the best atmospheres I have encountered in the US.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

The deadlock was broken by Lionel Messi for Argentina against Cape Verde with a triumph of technique as he showed exquisite touch to take a difficult pass on the run before, with little room to work with, he guided it into the top corner with his second touch.

In the same game, Sidny Cabral's extra-time equaliser was a wonderful moment. From the angle just inside the area, his right-foot curling drive into the top corner gave Emiliano Martinez no chance.

In the group stages, Kerim Alajbegovic's goal for Bosnia-Herzegovina against Qatar was a superb solo effort - a weaving run before a rising right-foot shot of high class.

What looked like a casual swing of Erling Haaland's left boot was in fact a perfectly placed finish with astonishing power to crown a match-winning performance for Norway against Brazil in the last 16.

A tight quarter-final between Argentina and Switzerland was broken open by a moment of genius from Julian Alvarez. The vastly under-rated attacker provided with a swerving right-footed drive from long range into the top corner in extra time.

Antonio Nusa's opener for Norway against Ivory Coast was perfection. A little shimmy in the area to feint away from a defender, then a precise curling finish into the top corner.

Phil McNulty, BBC Sport chief football writer

Johny Placide made a superb double save for Haiti against Morocco. It came first with his legs then he showed swift reactions to save one-handed to his left.

At the Azteca Stadium, Jordan Pickford had an outstanding game in England's memorable win over Mexico. His early save, diving low to his left to keep out Raul Jimenez's header, was a crucial moment of brilliance.

Orjan Nyland produced a remarkable stop for Norway against Brazil from his own defender Kristoffer Ajer, who inadvertently directed the ball goalwards. Nyland scrambled backwards showing remarkably anticipation and agility to leap and turn the ball against the post.

Aymen Dahmen showed stunning reactions for Tunisia against Japan from Takehiro Tomiyasu's close-range effort, somehow keeping the ball out as it did everything but cross the line.

There was a brilliant diving save by Patrick Beach for Australia in their group game with Turkey, diving away to his right to touch Abdulkerim Bardakci's rising 30-yard shot on to the post.

Iran's Alireza Beiranvand produced a remarkable save from Maxim de Cuyper of Belgium. Beiranvand could not reach Kevin de Bruyne's shot but somehow recovered his position and reacted to keep out a shot from inside the six-yard box.

Mbappe first to win Golden Boot twice as Rodri named best player

Lamine Yamal 'completes football' at 19 as Argentina betray Messi

World Cup is over, but the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Who made the difference for Spain? World Cup final player ratings
description: BBC Sport journalist Neil Johnston rates the players after the World Cup final between Spain and Argentina.
fullText:
Spain have won the World Cup after beating Argentina 1-0 in the final.

BBC Sport journalist Neil Johnston assesses how the players performed in New Jersey - and you can see how BBC Sport readers rated them at the bottom of the page too.

Unai Simon: Came charging outside his box in the opening minutes which almost led to disaster before recovering to help Spain add the World Cup to their European triumph of two-years ago. 7

Marc Cucurella: Drove forward on numerous occasions and went close with a rare first-half attempt which whistled just wide. 7

Aymeric Laporte: Has been a rock at the back throughout the tournament – and so he was once again as Spain recorded a seventh clean sheet in eight games. 7

Pau Cubarsi: Once again provided a calm presence in defence as he has done all tournament. Only the second player on record (from 1966) to complete 100-plus passes in a World Cup final. 8

Pedro Porro: Followed up on his solid display in the semi-final to play a part in the move that produced the winning goal. 7

Fabian Ruiz: Worked hard in the engine room as Argentina struggled to impose their authority. 7

Rodri: Another controlled midfield performance from the captain as he helped his country to their first World Cup triumph since 2010. 7

Alex Baena: Put himself about and made a nuisance of himself. 7

Dani Olmo: Created chances and made a nuisance of himself on another glorious day for Spanish football after the Euros success of 2024 and the Nations League triumph of 2023. 7

Lamine Yamal: Ends the tournament with a World Cup winners medal at the age of 19. Will there be more to come? Had the first chance of the final after finding space inside the box. 7

Mikel Oyarzabal: Has been influential upfront for Spain but was kept quiet for large parts. Ends a glorious tournament as Spain's leading scorer with five goals. 7

Pedri (62 mins): Came on to bring energy into the side. 7

Ferran Torres (62 mins): Injected pace into Spain's play and was first to react when the ball fell to him in extra-time to seal the biggest prize of all for his country. 8

Nico Williams (75 mins): What an impact. Williams has had to remain patient at this tournament and has been limited to a string of substitute appearances. Spain looked dangerous each time he had the ball at his feet and played a huge role in the goal. 9

Mikel Merino (75 mins): Has proved to be an impact substitute at this tournament and again played his part on a glorious day for Spain. 6

Eric Garcia (99 mins): Worked hard and put himself about after coming on in extra-time. 6

Martin Zubimendi (99 mins): Did what he needed to as Spain wrote another glorious chapter in their football history. 6

Listen: Football Daily - Spain win the World Cup

Emiliano Martinez: Produced a fine save to keep out Lamine Yamal's free-kick right at the end of normal time. 6

Nicolas Tagliafico: Helped frustrate Spain for large spells before Argentina's 10-men were undone. 6

Lisandro Martinez: Booked for a foul from behind on Mikel Oyarzabal. Then replaced by Nicolas Otamendi before half-time after picking up an injury. 5

Cristian Romero: Helped ensure Mikel Oyarzabal – Spain's top scorer at this World Cup – was quiet. Booked for dissent. 6

Gonzalo Montiel: Showed great reactions to save a goal after Dani Olmo's low cross. 6

Nico Gonzalez: Replaced at half-time after Argentina failed to register an attempt in the first half. 5

Alexis Mac Allister: Fortunate to escape a caution after a late challenge on Dani Olmo. 5

Enzo Fernandez: Shocking challenge on Pau Cubarsi which resulted in a second yellow card and dismissal – Argentina's first red card at a World Cup since 2006. 4

Rodrigo De Paul: Struggled to influence the game and was replaced midway through the second half. 5

Julian Alvarez: Did not impose his authority on a day that belonged to Spain. 5

Lionel Messi: If this was his final appearance on the World Cup stage, what a sad end for the legend. Struggled to seriously test the Spain defence and finished the tournament on eight goals – two behind France's Kylian Mbapppe. 6

Nicolas Otamendi (41 mins): Came on towards the end of the first half for Lisandro Martinez and tried to make things happen before Argentina were undone by Enzo Fernandez's red card. 5

Leandro Paredes (46 mins): He had only been on the pitch a few minutes when he was booked for shoving Rodri – and then was involved in ugly scenes at full time. 3

Nahuel Molina (50 mins): Disappointing end to his World Cup as Argentina came unstuck in the final. 5

Facundo Medina (70 mins): Unable to help his country as the best team won. 4

Giovanni Simeone (70 mins): Unable to make an impact as the Copa America winners struggled and Simeone could not get into get into the game. 4

Marcos Senesi (102 mins): Could not impact the game as Argentina conceded for the sixth straight game and lost the final. 4

After the opportunity to rate players has closed, the score displayed represents the average from all the submissions by BBC Sport users.

title: Antonelli near-perfect but too much drama for Hamilton - driver ratings
description: BBC Radio 5 Live F1 commentator Harry Benjamin rates how the drivers performed during the Belgian Grand Prix.
fullText:
Championship leader Kimi Antonelli returned to winning ways at the Belgian Grand Prix with victory from pole position at Spa-Francorchamps.

The Mercedes driver is now 45 points clear of Ferrari's Lewis Hamilton at the top of the standings after team-mate George Russell was involved in a collision with Hamilton, who finished fourth, on the opening lap.

Russell falls to third in the championship, 50 points adrift of Antonelli.

Here are my ratings for how the drivers performed across the whole weekend in Belgium.

A near-perfect weekend from Mercedes' championship leader. Topped two out of the three practice sessions and delivered pole position by more than three-tenths of a second. Recovered from an unfortunately timed virtual safety car to pass Charles Leclerc and win.

Maximised what Ferrari had and came closer than anyone to denying Antonelli the race win. Benefited from the virtual safety car but kept the Mercedes driver honest after losing the lead, although the contact with Oscar Piastri was an untidy moment.

A brilliant start briefly put him into the lead and he comfortably secured another podium for Red Bull. Qualified on the front row and drove a strong race, but ultimately couldn't match Antonelli when it mattered.

Plenty of pace but far too much drama. Recovered from his heavy final-practice crash and the penalty for hitting George Russell to finish fourth, but an overall messy Sunday prevented this from being rated as a strong weekend.

Had damage after the contact with Leclerc but remained in the fight and brought home a useful fifth place. Losing fourth to Hamilton late on capped a weekend in which he never quite looked as comfortable as McLaren team-mate Lando Norris.

One of the drives of the race. Charged from 21st on the grid to sixth with an aggressive strategy and held off Norris at the finish to secure a huge result for Red Bull.

Qualifying third showed the McLaren had real pace, although his final Q3 mistake cost him the chance to challenge Antonelli. Recovered well from his grid penalty but a long first stint and slow pit stop left him just short of Hadjar.

Another excellent weekend for the Audi driver. Reached Q3, stayed out of trouble and converted eighth on the grid into a second consecutive points finish.

A strong qualifying performance put him eighth and he delivered another mature drive into the points. Racing Bulls had one of the quickest midfield cars, but Lindblad still had to hold his nerve to make the most of it.

Excellent work to claim the final point. Produced a decisive move during the three-way battle with Pierre Gasly and Liam Lawson and once again got the better of his more experienced Alpine team-mate.

Antonelli wins to extend lead after Russell crashes out

Mercedes driver scout Lagrue joining Red Bull

The crash in practice compromised the start of his weekend and he narrowly missed out on points on Sunday. Qualified ahead of Colapinto but losing 10th to his team-mate made it a disappointing result.

Looked set for a good haul of points after running sixth during the opening stages. His attempt to pass Gasly allowed Colapinto through and he eventually slipped to 12th, behind Racing Bulls team-mate Lindblad.

Was involved in the fight for the final point but couldn't convert it into a top-10 finish. Being comfortably out-qualified and outscored by Audi team-mate Bortoleto takes the shine off his afternoon.

Missed a place in Q2 by only 0.033 seconds but never really threatened the points in the race. A largely anonymous weekend for the Haas driver.

Williams lacked competitiveness throughout the weekend but Albon at least led the team home. Finished ahead of Carlos Sainz after starting behind him, although 15th offered little reward.

His grid penalty and early damage made this a difficult race to judge. Was unhappy with Williams' decision to change his front wing and eventually finished behind team-mate Albon.

A poor weekend from the Haas driver. Qualified 18th and separate first-lap incidents with Sainz and team-mate Bearman resulted in a puncture and left him near the back.

The only Cadillac driver to reach the chequered flag but there wasn't much else to celebrate. Qualified ahead of team-mate Sergio Perez and kept the car clean, although he finished a lap down.

Another grim weekend for Aston Martin. Started last after a grid penalty and finished two laps behind the winner, with little evidence that the car was capable of fighting anyone.

Qualified on the final row before his penalty and retired with a suspected gearbox problem. The machinery did him no favours, but there were few positives before his race came to an end.

Outqualified by team-mate Bottas and retired after reporting a suspension problem. Another difficult weekend for Perez and Cadillac with virtually no opportunity to show competitive pace.

A desperately unfortunate end to a troubling weekend. Did well to qualify fourth despite his unexplained straight-line speed deficit, before a battery problem left him vulnerable to Hamilton and the resulting collision ended his race on the first lap.

title: Meet the Team NI cycling squad
description: BBC Sport NI looks at the five members of the Team NI cycling squad.
fullText:
Five cyclists will make their Commonwealth Games debuts for Team NI in Glasgow this summer.

Erin Creighton was a reserve at the 2024 Olympics after helping Team Ireland's women qualify for the team pursuit event for the first time.

The 22-year-old claimed the 2022 Irish junior road race title and finished fourth in that year's Junior World Championships elimination race and is set to compete in the scratch, points and elimination races.

Emma Jeffers began in BMX racing before making the switch to road racing, with the 21-year-old since claiming two sprint victories at the Gracia in the Czech Republic, while also enjoying a victory at the National Circuit Championships.

Esther Wong currently rides for Team Farto – Kiroot and has raced on road, track and cyclo-cross circuits.

The versatile 20-year-old has enjoyed success at junior and U23 level, with top-15 finishes at the National Championships Ireland WE road race and the Région Pays de la Loire Tour - Féminin.

Matthew Dobbins has competed internationally for Ireland in a range of road cycling competitions and the 25-year-old enjoyed a sixth-place stage two finish at last year's Tour of Kosovo.

Chris Burns is a C2 para-cyclist and has enjoyed recent national and international success including a triple podium finish at the German Open Paracycling Track Championships.

title: I've been a World Cup official - I promise you there is no favouritism
description: Darren Cann speaks to BBC Sport about the conspiracy theories around the World Cup and what referees really think.
fullText:
According to many fans, social media timelines and corners of the internet, Fifa wants Argentina and Lionel Messi to win the World Cup - and the officials are all in on it.

As referees, we are forced to deal with the idea that we are part of some kind of conspiracy theory all the time.

And it is nonsense. I can't stress this strongly enough.

I was lucky enough to be an assistant referee at two World Cups, in 2010 and 2014, as part of a team which featured referee Howard Webb, with Michael Mullarkey running the other line.

We were independent, and we made our own decisions. I can assure you this has not changed.

The referees are from all four corners of the globe, all from different football associations, separate confederations, and Fifa effectively borrows their services for six weeks.

They are completely distinct from Fifa, in a separate training camp.

I'm sorry to disappoint any conspiracy theorists, but I can promise you there aren't any clandestine meetings between Fifa's executive committee and the referees.

We don't see any of the suits at all.

You are basically working every day - physical training, watching clips, debriefing the matches from the previous day.

They are full-on days. There's not a lot of downtime, and once you have completed one assignment, you are preparing for the next one.

The suggestion that referees are in on some kind of masterplan to make sure Messi gets to the final is just not true, obviously.

There is, apparently, a rumour that Fifa wanted an Argentina v Spain final to create a passing of the baton between Messi and Lamine Yamal.

I personally had no idea that there was even a photograph of Messi with Lamine Yamal when he was a baby. I suspect that might also be true for many of the referees at this World Cup.

Yes, as referees we might make mistakes. This is human nature. And sometimes it might feel like the decisions are going against your country or club. But that's just the result of a fast-moving game with human beings at the centre of it - we are not being directed to create a pre-determined outcome.

Nor do we referee by the name on a player's back.

Howard and I did a Champions League game some years ago. It was Barcelona at home to Bayern Munich. A huge Champions League game.

Messi went down in the penalty area, we didn't award a penalty and the game continued.

We got back to England the next day, and reviewed the match and the footage as we did after every game.

In hindsight it probably was a penalty - we probably did get it wrong - but we called it honestly.

You just make the decision, irrespective of the teams and which players are involved.

You're making a judgement as to whether a player has been fouled. You often don't even have time to even look to see who the players involved are. You're just seeing a red shirt against a blue shirt.

Conspiracy theories and accusations of bias cause real damage and lead to abuse for referees. It can be difficult to deal with.

Pierluigi Collina, who oversees all the referees at the World Cup, had to issue a defence of his referees after Argentina's 3-2 win over Egypt in the last 16. He underlined their integrity and independence.

Egypt had made strong accusations that referee Francois Letexier and his team had deliberately favoured Argentina. That they had in some way been instructed to go against their natural instincts.

Collina was right to say that narrative can lead to threats against a referee's family, and I've seen it before myself.

I'm very lucky that it did not happen to me personally, but I think it's partly because I'm an assistant referee and not a referee.

We officiated the final between Spain and the Netherlands in 2010. A proud day for us all.

The game, which Spain won 1-0, was extremely challenging to officiate. There were 14 yellow cards and Dutch defender Johnny Heitinga was sent off. Nigel de Jong could have seen red too.

There was a lot of criticism from Netherlands coach Bert van Marwijk. That is what you have to deal with just for making genuine decisions.

Sometimes it is worse than that. Our own English referees at this World Cup, Michael Oliver and Anthony Taylor, have received threats in past years.

It is totally unacceptable and it is heightened by these unfounded whispers which question a referee's impartiality.

I support England, I love England, and obviously we lost to Argentina.

The referee was, in my opinion, a little bit too lenient in the first 25 minutes. But ultimately, the reason why we're not in the World Cup final is not down to the referee.

Really, it's frustrating when you hear about conspiracy theories.

I can't think of many other professions in the world where there is one person with all that pressure on them.

The referee is making multi-million-pound decisions - and they have got one second to make their mind up.

Mistakes have been made on the pitch, of course they have. But they do not add up to a conspiracy.

In thr group stage, I think Germany's goal against Ecuador should have been disallowed for a high foot. Ghana could have been awarded a penalty against England for a mistimed challenge by Ezri Konsa. And when they were 1-0 up against Scotland, Brazil scored a goal that should have been allowed to stand, but was ruled out.

There have been 103 matches so far. That's effectively 10 rounds of Premier League football, isn't it? So, of course, there are going to be errors made. It's almost impossible to go through that many games without one.

Slovenian referee Slavko Vincic will take charge of Sunday's final.

It will be the pinnacle of his career. You only need to look at his emotional reaction when Collina read out his name at the referee briefing the other day.

Having been fortunate enough to officiate a World Cup final myself, I can assure you that that Vincic will not care one jot who wins.

All he'll be hoping for is what every referee hopes for, and that is to come off the field without any incorrect decisions that impact the result.

All these conspiracy theories are bogus.

The only thing in your mind, I assure you, is getting the decisions correct.

Darren Cann was speaking to BBC Sport's Dale Johnson.

title: How did England players rate during the World Cup?
description: BBC Sport England reporter Alex Howell assess how the Three Lions' players fared in North America.
fullText:
England finished the World Cup by beating France 6-4 in an extraordinary third-place play-off.

BBC Sport England reporter Alex Howell rates how the players did throughout the tournament.

Jordan Pickford: The Everton goalkeeper is undoubtedly England's number one and has proven he can deal with being on the big stage. However, this was not Pickford's best tournament and he produced some erratic moments. His best performance came in the semi-final loss to Argentina, but he will question himself over Enzo Fernandez's goal. 6

Dean Henderson: England's second-choice goalkeeper came to the tournament in form. Henderson started the bronze-medal match against France, producing an excellent save to deny Kylian Mbappe but conceding four goals. 6

James Trafford: Came to the tournament as third choice and did not feature but travelling to a World Cup is good experience. N/A

Ezri Konsa: The Aston Villa defender featured in every game and continued to show he has the quality for international football. A starting centre-back and also played at right-back in the win over Norway in the quarter-finals. He ended the tournament with a goal in the third-place play-off. 7

John Stones: It was considered a gamble by Thomas Tuchel to take Stones considering his fitness issues. He started the first game, had some shaky moments and dropped out for the next couple. But he regrouped and started the quarter-final win over Norway and semi-final defeat by Argentina. The 32-year-old showed his quality but was caught under the ball for Lautaro Martinez's winner. 7

Marc Guehi: A staple of the England backline at major tournaments. Guehi did not start the opening game against Croatia but when he came in he showed his usual calmness and quality. Guehi put in an excellent performance against Manchester City team-mate Erling Haaland in the quarter-final against Norway and performed well until England went out. 8

Trevoh Chalobah: The Chelsea defender was called up as a replacement for Tino Livramento. It was a decision that prompted a lot of debate as it was felt it left England short in the full-back areas. Chalobah was brought on in the closing stages of the win over France for his only minutes of the tournament. 5

Jarell Quansah: This was Quansah's first major tournament and he was arguably a surprise pick in the 26-man squad. But the Bayer Leverkusen defender did show why Tuchel was so keen to have him as a full-back option. Quansah started well against Panama before getting injured, then did likewise against Mexico before he was sent off. He returned for the third-place play-off, and has a bright international future. 6

Dan Burn: The Newcastle defender's inclusion was the subject of much debate but his performance against Mexico underlined the reasoning. Burn's aerial prowess gave England a way to see out games, though Tuchel turned to that tactic too early when trying to see out a win against Argentina. 6.5

Nico O'Reilly: This was the 21-year-old's first tournament and he grew into it after a nervy opening match. O'Reilly only became England's first-choice left-back in November, and to go from that to a World Cup and start five of the eight matches is very impressive. O'Reilly is very good on the ball but could improve defensively. 6.5

Reece James: The Chelsea captain said it was "boring" when people discussed his fitness, but the first-choice right-back missed three games after sustaining a hamstring injury against Ghana. He recovered to play in the quarter-final and semi-final, and showed his quality. 6.5

Djed Spence: The Tottenham full-back was one of England's breakout players of the tournament. Spence's ability to play at both left-back and right-back is extremely valuable in tournament football. He is a very good one-v-one defender and his pace gives an outlet to the team. He produced two of his best performances in the quarter-final and semi-final. Spence also played well against France, winning a penalty. 8

Declan Rice: The Arsenal midfielder had to battle with his fitness throughout the tournament. He put his body on the line while nursing a hamstring problem and started the quarter-final after illness. He imposed himself on games physically but this was perhaps not his best tournament in possession. Rice captained the side in the third-place play-off, scoring and providing an assist. 7.5

Jude Bellingham: England's talisman. Bellingham went to another level during this World Cup and showed how important he is and will be in the future. He has superstar quality with the mentality to match. He scored seven goals - the last an excellent individual effort against France - and now holds the record for the most goals by an Englishman at a World Cup. 9

Jordan Henderson: The Brentford midfielder became the first Englishman to feature at four World Cups, but his tournament ended in bizarre fashion. Henderson fell over an advertising hoarding and broke his arm after the win over Mexico. He stayed with the group after surgery but was never a viable option after that. 4

Elliot Anderson: It was a big ask for Anderson to be the starting number six for England. He did it well, starting every game apart from the third-place play-off. His best game in possession was against Argentina, but if England are to get over the line in a major tournament they will need him to be more of a controller. 7

Kobbie Mainoo: The only outfield player to not get any minutes, with the Football Association saying he missed the third-place play-off with an injury. N/A

Bukayo Saka: The Arsenal winger came into the tournament nursing an Achilles issue and admitted it would be a "gamble" for him. He showed his quality in flashes but wasn't able to show his top level consistently, though did score a hat-trick in the third-place play-off against France. 7.5

Morgan Rogers: Was the focus of much attention coming into the tournament and played in every position behind the striker. He played a big part in both the quarter-final and semi-final, when his assist for Anthony Gordon had England fans dreaming. Rogers has quality and his physicality is a real asset. 7.5

Anthony Gordon: The Barcelona winger admitted he had a slow start to the tournament but grew into it to have some massive moments. He followed two assists in the win over DR Congo by earning the penalty for what proved to be the winner in Mexico, then produced an assist for the equaliser against Norway and scored the opener against Argentina in the semi-final. Gordon can be pleased with his impact. 8

Eberechi Eze: It was a quiet tournament for Eze but he still offers something different off the bench. He came on as a half-time substitute against Norway but things didn't work out. He was given a start in the third-place play-off and performed well in midfield, setting up Saka for England's fourth. 6.5

Ollie Watkins: The Aston Villa striker was only given six minutes against Panama before being brought on at half-time against France. It could be argued his ability to run in behind the defence should have been used against Argentina. 5

Noni Madueke: His call-up was one that prompted most discussion but his unique skillset means he is an asset. His constant direct running and dribbling mean he is highly valued by Tuchel. Madueke won the penalty in opening match with Croatia for England's first goal of the tournament. 6

Ivan Toney: The Al Ahli striker was a surprise inclusion in the squad, clearly brought for his penalty-taking ability. Toney was thrown on in the dying moments as England chased an equaliser against Argentina, then started the third-placed play-off. 6

Marcus Rashford: A mixed tournament for Rashford, who scored in England's opening game off the bench. He was substituted during the match with DR Congo, then used for one minute as England tried to salvage the match against Argentina. He produced some bright moments against France - and an assist for Saka. 6.5

Harry Kane: Another tournament in which the England captain delivered goals. His six before the quarter-final had England fans dreaming it could be their year. Kane also provided an assist for Bellingham in the win over Mexico, but that match and the Norway game going to extra time seemed to take a lot out of him and he was not at his best against Argentina. 8.5

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

False nines or firing forwards - England's potential life after Kane

title: Rooney, Hart and Richards row down Hudson River
description: Wayne Rooney rows down the Hudson River with fellow BBC pundits Joe Hart and Micah Richards.
fullText:
Wayne Rooney is a man of his word.

Turn the clock back to the last 16 of the World Cup, when the former England striker pledged to row down the River Mersey if Norway beat Brazil.

Norway did exactly that, beating the five-time champions 2-1, so BBC Sport pundit Rooney confirmed live on air that he would take on the challenge in the US.

He and fellow pundits Joe Hart and Micah Richards swapped their microphones for oars and took to the Hudson River in New York.

"I loved it," Rooney said. "The fans were brilliant. So we've done it out of respect for the fans."

Norway fans' viking row became a sensation at the World Cup, and even Norway striker Erling Haaland was keen to see Rooney in action.

"Wayne, I expect you to go out on a rowing trip," he said.

Norway were knocked out by England in the quarter-finals.

Rooney, Hart and Richards will be back on dry land alongside Gabby Logan for Sunday's World Cup final (20:00 BST), which will be available to watch live on BBC One and BBC iPlayer.

England play France in the World Cup third-place match on Saturday at 22:00, also live on BBC One and BBC iPlayer.

Watch live on BBC One and BBC iPlayer (UHD coverage available) and listen to build-up and full match commentary on BBC Radio 5 Live and BBC Sounds. Follow TV and radio coverage, plus live text updates, in-play video clips, post-match highlights and analysis on the BBC Sport website and app.

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Inside Iraola's first week at Liverpool and big decisions he faces
description: New Liverpool manager Andoni Iraola has lots of decisions to make ahead of the new season. BBC Sport gives an insight into his first week in the job.
fullText:
It is always difficult to know what to make of pre-season and how much of it is worth reading into before the real stuff begins.

Iago Aspas scored and got an assist for Liverpool on his debut in a pre-season friendly in 2013 but only scored one competitive goal - against Oldham in the FA Cup - before returning to Spain the next summer.

For Andoni Iraola though, this has been the week where life at Liverpool has really started. It's the time to plant the seeds for what he and Liverpool hope will be the start of a fruitful era.

On Monday, the new head coach delivered his first press conference at the AXA Training Centre, a day before the bulk of the first-team squad returned to training.

A positive Iraola took the opportunity to insist that Liverpool need more signings, and that will certainly accelerate now that the World Cup is concluding. Nevertheless, the news of Dominik Szoboszlai signing a five-year deal is certainly an important development.

Of the many decisions Iraola will have to make, appointing a vice-captain will be key. Virgil van Dijk played every minute for Liverpool in the Premier League last season before playing for Netherlands at the World Cup.

At the age of 35 and with his contract expiring next summer, Liverpool must look ahead for when, in all likelihood, they will need a new skipper.

In terms of seniority, goalkeeper Alisson Becker would be an option but his contract also expires next summer, so Szoboszlai is the standout candidate.

"I'm 25. Mo left, Robbo left, so our vice-captain left and a club legend as well. Both of them are club legends. We're going to miss them for sure and now someone else has to step up and if it's me, I'm ready to do it," said Szoboszlai, who captains Hungary.

At the Kirkby training base, the players spent the first couple of days undergoing the usual individual tests before getting on the pitch with the ball on Thursday and Friday.

Iraola is heavily involved in these sessions and often says "bravo" when he sees something he likes. The intensity is evident to see, particularly in the training videos uploaded by Liverpool's official channels.

Iraola and his coaching staff spend most of their day at the training ground - with a major emphasis on assessing the capabilities of the current squad.

The new head coach has told players that he's viewing them as new signings. Harvey Elliott returned a week earlier than expected while Curtis Jones looks like "he has had a great summer", according to one source. Jeremy Jacquet, who has been given the number five shirt, is settling in nicely and has recovered from his shoulder injury.

On the whole, the players have returned in "good nick", with medical staff impressed. When it comes to endurance, under Jurgen Klopp the players would do a lactate test while it was a six-minute race test under Slot.

Iraola's fitness test is slightly different - players move on a pitch with diagonals and cones to replicate changes of direction with high intensity.

"It's deceptive as your legs just go," defender Joe Gomez can be heard saying to Jones.

Those at the training ground say there is certainly a youthful vibe to the squad, with eight players on World Cup duty yet to join. As one source puts it: "This really is a chance for a new group to emerge before more of the seniors return."

"Definitely there are going to be changes," Iraola told Stan Sports in an interview. "A lot of senior players, players who have won everything here have left and we need other players to not replace them but assume (more responsibility) in terms of inside and outside the pitch."

Coming into a club the size of Liverpool has certainly been different for Iraola but there is a general respect around the building for the 44-year-old and the job he did at Bournemouth.

If his first week is anything to go by, Iraola is confident and certainly comfortable but not necessarily box office. Perhaps one way to put it for now is that his presence won't be felt yet like it was with a big character like Klopp.

Those who know him well believe he won't change his ways and Iraola said as much in his introductory press conference.

Come Monday afternoon, Liverpool will be flying across the Atlantic to Chicago, where they will be based for their pre-season tour that also includes fixtures in Nashville and New York.

That trip will offer Iraola the first real chance of working with an expanded group of players, with most of the remaining first team squad expected to join up, and a range of academy players also travelling, including 15-year-old Josh Abe.

Either side of the three scheduled friendlies, where most will get playing time, double training sessions are scheduled in the heat. Both Iraola and his squad will know that from now on, the pressure will only heat up too.
